request.question: Whom did Rosko Gee play with on their albums "When the Eagle Flies" (1974) and "The Last Great Traffic Jam" (2005)?

title: Saw Delight
The Last Great Traffic Jam
Steve Winwood
When the Eagle Flies
Rosko Gee
Probka
Traffic wave
Gold (Traffic album)
China National Highway 110 traffic jam
Aizawl Khawpui Traffic Jam Hi
sentences: Saw Delight is the ninth Can studio album, and features two new band members who were ex-members of the band Traffic, Rosko Gee and Rebop Kwaku Baah, with Can's bassist Holger Czukay giving up the bass in favour of experimental effects.
The Last Great Traffic Jam is a live album and DVD from the rock band Traffic.
The album was recorded from the 1994 Traffic reunion concert tour.
Stephen Lawrence "Steve" Winwood (born 12 May 1948) is an English musician whose genres include rock, blue-eyed soul, rhythm and blues, blues rock, pop rock, and jazz.
Though primarily a vocalist and keyboardist, Winwood also plays bass guitar, drums, acoustic and electric guitar, mandolin, violin, and other strings.
When the Eagle Flies is the seventh studio album released by English rock band Traffic, in 1974.
It was the final album released by the band until their 1994 reunion "Far from Home".
The album featured Jim Capaldi on drums, keyboards and vocals; Rosko Gee on bass guitar; Steve Winwood on guitar, keyboards, and vocals; and Chris Wood on flute and saxophone.
(Percussionist Rebop Kwaku Baah was fired prior to the album's completion, but his contributions are heard on two of its tracks.)
The album uses a broader variety of keyboard instruments than previous Traffic albums, adding Moog to their repertoire.

Rosko Gee is a Jamaican bassist, who has played with the English band Traffic on their albums "When the Eagle Flies" (1974) and "The Last Great Traffic Jam" (2005); with Go featuring Stomu Yamashta, Steve Winwood, Michael Shrieve, Klaus Schulze and Al Di Meola; and with the German band Can, along with former Traffic percussionist Rebop Kwaku Baah, appearing on the albums "Saw Delight", "Out of Reach" and "Can".
He toured with Can in 1977, whereas Rebop did not.
He also provided vocals for some of the band's songs during this period.
"Probka" (Russian Cyrillic: Пробка; English translation: Traffic Jam) is a song by Latvian singer Intars Busulis, and was the Latvian entry at the Eurovision Song Contest 2009 in Moscow, Russia.
The song competed in the second semi-final of the contest on 14 May 2009 but failed to reach the final, having finished last with 7 points.
The song is unusually rhythmically complex for a Eurovision song, with the main verse section played in the unusual time signature of 7/4, and constant changes in tempo.
Busulis described "Probka" as "a song about a traffic jam in all senses and meanings.
We all have experienced in our lives that we just hurry, rush somewhere and at the certain moment there is a stop, jam or "probka".
That’s it – there is no way out, we are stuck!
This is an appropriate moment to think if it was worth it to run and hurry at all."
Traffic waves, also called stop waves or traffic shocks, are traveling disturbances in the distribution of cars on a highway.
Traffic waves travel backwards relative to the cars themselves.
Relative to a fixed spot on the road the wave can move with, or against the traffic, or even be stationary (when the waves moves away from the traffic with exactly the same speed as the traffic).
Traffic waves are a type of traffic jam.
A deeper understanding of traffic waves is a goal of the physical study of traffic flow, in which traffic itself can often be seen using techniques similar to those used in fluid dynamics.
Traffic Gold is a two-disc 2005 compilation album by the psychedelic rock band Traffic.
It contains at least one song from each album except "On the Road", "Far from Home", and "The Last Great Traffic Jam".

The China National Highway 110 traffic jam was a recurring massive traffic jam that began to form on August 14, 2010, mostly on China National Highway 110 (G110) and Beijing–Tibet expressway (G6), in Hebei and Inner Mongolia.
The traffic jam slowed down thousands of vehicles for more than 100 km and lasted for more than ten days.
Many drivers were able to move their vehicles only 1 km (0.6 mi) per day, and some drivers reported being stuck in the traffic jam for five days.
It is considered to be one of the longest traffic jams by some media.
Aizawl Khawpui Traffic Jam Hi is a 2017 Indian Mizo-language documentary film narrated, produced and directed by C. Hmingthangvunga.
The film tells about the traffic jam in Aizawl, the capital of Mizoram State in India.

candidate.summarize1.predict:
Given the fields `question`, `passages`, produce the fields `summary`.

Task: Compress the grounded first-hop evidence into a single concise, retrieval-oriented intermediate state to guide the next-hop retrieval. Do not produce the final answer or perform any new retrieval here.

Required content of `summary` (single field):
- A short header identifying the primary topic(s) found in the passages (1 line).
- A concise enumeration of the concrete facts supported verbatim by the provided passages: list entity mentions and directly supported factual spans (dates, years, titles, roles). Use very short quoted fragments (<= ~15 words) only when those words appear verbatim in the passages. Keep to facts present in the passages; do not add or infer new facts.
- Explicitly expose any ambiguity or missing information that blocks answering: if multiple candidate entities or page variants appear, enumerate those candidates and label the name as ambiguous; if a required target fact is missing or ambiguous, label it as the missing fact and identify the bridge entity that connects this hop to the next.
- Prioritized next-hop retrieval directives (1–4 items): concrete page titles or disambiguation pages to fetch, explicit article sections to inspect (e.g., lede/infobox, "Career", "Publication", "History"), or tight search keywords. Order them by priority and be specific (include possible disambiguation terms or section names).

Formatting and style constraints:
- Keep the entire `summary` concise: a short header plus 1–4 brief sentences or 2–6 short bullet lines in total.
- Preserve the habit of extracting and citing concrete facts verbatim from the passages (names, dates, passage labels).
- Do not output a final answer, comparison, or any numeric fact that purports to resolve the question here. Even if the passages fully and unambiguously contain all required facts, present that evidence and explicitly state "no additional retrieval needed," but defer producing any final answer to a downstream component.
- If a name or term has multiple distinct variants in the passages, do not pick one without disambiguating evidence; enumerate candidates instead.
- Do not invent or add facts not present in the supplied passages. Avoid long verbatim quotations; prefer short extracts (<= ~15 words) or paraphrase.

Output expectations: produce exactly one field `summary` containing the concise, retrieval-oriented summary that meets the above requirements.
candidate.create_query_hop2.predict:
Given the fields `question`, `summary_1`, produce the fields `query`.

Produce exactly one compact, retrieval-focused second-hop query in the field `query`. Use only the provided `question` and `summary_1` as inputs. Do not include any explanation, answer, fallback text, lists, or additional fields — the output must be a single one-line query string.

Decision templates (choose the single template that matches the missing evidence implied by summary_1):
- Connector between two named items: emit a short interrogative naming both items and requesting the linking evidence (e.g., artist album recording) in one line. Retain the visible item tokens from the inputs.
- Metadata for a single anchor title: emit a compact keyword-style query consisting of the anchor plus brief attribute keywords (for example: artist genre).
- Temporal or aggregate fact: include the year or season token plus the entity type and a short cue such as season count.

Strict formatting rules:
- Emit exactly one line and exactly one string in `query` containing only the query text.
- Keep the query compact (aim for ≤ 10–12 words/tokens) and free of punctuation or explanatory clauses.
- Preserve visible tokens from `question` and `summary_1` (title words, years, or entity names) to anchor retrieval.
- Do not answer, conclude, enumerate candidates, assert hidden facts, or include user-facing instructions.

Produce the `query` field now.
candidate.summarize2.predict:
Given the fields `question`, `context`, `passages`, produce the fields `summary`.

Instructions (must follow exactly):
- Emit a single field named `summary` containing only 1–3 concise sentences that integrate the first-hop `context` and the second-hop `passages` into a grounded post-retrieval state.
- If the `context` already contains a concise answer sentence, keep that sentence as the first sentence of `summary` unless the `passages` clearly contradict it; append supporting sentence(s) after it rather than replacing it.
- Preserve the primary answer name(s) exactly as they appear in the inputs (context or passages) unless those names clearly conflict with stronger passage content.
- When one or more `passages` supply short biographical, membership, partnership, or corroborating facts about a named entity from the `context`, append at most one brief supporting sentence per relevant named person. Each such supporting sentence should:
  - begin with the entity's canonical name as shown in the passages (if available),
  - paraphrase up to one or two key attributes present in the passages (examples: nationality, award/winner status, current affiliation/team, partner identity, founding/formation fact, birth date), and
  - avoid long verbatim copying (prefer paraphrase; use short quotes only when a brief exact phrase from a passage is necessary for clarity).
- If the question requires naming both members of a partnership or collaborator pair, name both people in the summary and, if passages provide partner-specific attributes, include one brief attribute for each as separate short clauses or sentences if needed.
- Do not invent facts not present in the provided passages. If passages conflict on a specific attribute, do not synthesize a new reconciled fact; instead prefer the passage that most directly addresses the question or omit the disputed attribute (or state necessary ambiguity only when needed to avoid a false claim).
- Keep the `summary` compact and focused: avoid extraneous biography, discography/chart data, or unrelated commentary. When `context` is absent or is not a concise answer, produce a 1–2 sentence integrated summary naming the relevant entity(ies) and including at most one brief paraphrased attribute drawn from the passages.

Output requirement: exactly one JSON field `summary` whose value is the integrated 1–3 sentence summary.
candidate.final_answer.predict:
Given the fields `question`, `summary_1`, `summary_2`, produce the fields `answer`.

Instructions:
- Use only `summary_1` and `summary_2` as the evidential basis for the final decision; do not add new facts or external information.
- First determine whether the question is a binary (yes/no) proposition and whether both `summary_1` and `summary_2` clearly and unambiguously support the same binary outcome.
- If BOTH summaries unambiguously assert the same binary conclusion, emit exactly one lowercase token as the entire content of `answer`: use "yes" for an affirmative conclusion and "no" for a negative conclusion. The `answer` field must contain that single token and nothing else (no punctuation, no extra whitespace, no explanation).
- Prefer `summary_2` as the decisive statement when it clarifies or restates `summary_1`, but still require that both summaries support the same binary judgment before emitting the single-token `answer`.
- If the summaries conflict, if either summary hedges, is ambiguous, silent about the binary relation, or otherwise fails to clearly agree, do NOT apply the single-token rule; instead produce the module's normal fallback/unresolved output for `answer`.
- Always remain truthful to the visible summaries and do not alter their factual conclusions.

response.hop1_query: Whom did Rosko Gee play with on their albums "When the Eagle Flies" (1974) and "The Last Great Traffic Jam" (2005)?
hop1_docs: Rosko Gee | Rosko Gee is a Jamaican bassist, who has played with the English band Traffic on their albums "When the Eagle Flies" (1974) and "The Last Great Traffic Jam" (2005); with Go featuring Stomu Yamashta, Steve Winwood, Michael Shrieve, Klaus Schulze and Al Di Meola; and with the German band Can, along with former Traffic percussionist Rebop Kwaku Baah, appearing on the albums "Saw Delight", "Out of Reach" and "Can".  He toured with Can in 1977, whereas Rebop did not.  He also provided vocals for some of the band's songs during this period.

When the Eagle Flies | When the Eagle Flies is the seventh studio album released by English rock band Traffic, in 1974.  It was the final album released by the band until their 1994 reunion "Far from Home".  The album featured Jim Capaldi on drums, keyboards and vocals; Rosko Gee on bass guitar; Steve Winwood on guitar, keyboards, and vocals; and Chris Wood on flute and saxophone.  (Percussionist Rebop Kwaku Baah was fired prior to the album's completion, but his contributions are heard on two of its tracks.)  The album uses a broader variety of keyboard instruments than previous Traffic albums, adding Moog to their repertoire.
The Last Great Traffic Jam | The Last Great Traffic Jam is a live album and DVD from the rock band Traffic.  The album was recorded from the 1994 Traffic reunion concert tour.

Gold (Traffic album) | Traffic Gold is a two-disc 2005 compilation album by the psychedelic rock band Traffic.  It contains at least one song from each album except "On the Road", "Far from Home", and "The Last Great Traffic Jam".

Far from Home (Traffic album) | Far From Home, released in 1994, is the eighth and final studio album by the rock band Traffic, and the first in two decades since the release of "When the Eagle Flies" in 1974.  It was recorded at a large house called Woodstock, outside Kilcoole to the south of Dublin, and mixed at the Chateau Miraval in Correns, southern France.

Saw Delight | Saw Delight is the ninth Can studio album, and features two new band members who were ex-members of the band Traffic, Rosko Gee and Rebop Kwaku Baah, with Can's bassist Holger Czukay giving up the bass in favour of experimental effects.

To Whom It May Concern (Bee Gees album) | To Whom It May Concern is a 1972 album by the Bee Gees.  Released in October 1972, it was the follow-up, and continued the melancholic and personal sound of its predecessor "Trafalgar".  The album was recognized as "a farewell to the old Bee Gees" as the album marked the end of an era for the group in several ways: it was their last album to be recorded at IBC Studios, in London, their last with conductor and arranger Bill Shepherd who had guided them since 1967, and their last under their first contract with Robert Stigwood.  Some of the songs were old ones finished up or rewritten for the occasion (in the case of "I Can Bring Love").  "To Whom It May Concern" has sold approximately 350,000 copies worldwide.
hop1_titles: To Whom It May Concern (Bee Gees album)
response.summary_1:
Primary topic: Rosko Gee and the band Traffic (albums "When the Eagle Flies" (1974) and "The Last Great Traffic Jam" (2005)).

Concrete facts supported by the passages:
- "Rosko Gee is a Jamaican bassist" (passage 1).
- He "has played with the English band Traffic on their albums 'When the Eagle Flies' (1974) and 'The Last Great Traffic Jam' (2005)" (passage 1).
- "When the Eagle Flies" (1974) "featured Jim Capaldi on drums, keyboards and vocals; Rosko Gee on bass guitar; Steve Winwood on guitar, keyboards, and vocals; and Chris Wood on flute and saxophone." (passage 2).
- "Percussionist Rebop Kwaku Baah was fired prior to the album's completion, but his contributions are heard on two of its tracks." (passage 2).
- "'The Last Great Traffic Jam' is a live album and DVD from the rock band Traffic" recorded "from the 1994 Traffic reunion concert tour." (passage 3).

Ambiguities / missing facts blocking a complete answer:
- Missing fact: explicit personnel/credits for "The Last Great Traffic Jam" (album/DVD) are not listed in the passages — bridge entity: the album "The Last Great Traffic Jam".
- The word "their" in the question could be ambiguous, but passages identify "their" as the band Traffic.

Prioritized next-hop retrieval directives:
1. Fetch the page "The Last Great Traffic Jam" — inspect lede/infobox and "Personnel"/"Credits" or DVD credits (highest priority).
2. Fetch the page "When the Eagle Flies" — inspect "Personnel" / infobox to confirm listed musicians (verify passage 2).
3. Fetch "Traffic (band)" — inspect "Members", "1994 reunion", or "Lineup" sections to confirm reunion personnel and Rosko Gee's participation in the 1994 tour.
4. If needed, search: "Traffic Last Great Traffic Jam personnel Rosko Gee Steve Winwood Jim Capaldi Chris Wood DVD credits" for concert DVD credit listings.

Gold (Traffic album) | Traffic Gold is a two-disc 2005 compilation album by the psychedelic rock band Traffic.  It contains at least one song from each album except "On the Road", "Far from Home", and "The Last Great Traffic Jam".

China National Highway 110 traffic jam | The China National Highway 110 traffic jam was a recurring massive traffic jam that began to form on August 14, 2010, mostly on China National Highway 110 (G110) and Beijing–Tibet expressway (G6), in Hebei and Inner Mongolia.  The traffic jam slowed down thousands of vehicles for more than 100 km and lasted for more than ten days.  Many drivers were able to move their vehicles only 1 km (0.6 mi) per day, and some drivers reported being stuck in the traffic jam for five days.  It is considered to be one of the longest traffic jams by some media.

When the Eagle Flies | When the Eagle Flies is the seventh studio album released by English rock band Traffic, in 1974.  It was the final album released by the band until their 1994 reunion "Far from Home".  The album featured Jim Capaldi on drums, keyboards and vocals; Rosko Gee on bass guitar; Steve Winwood on guitar, keyboards, and vocals; and Chris Wood on flute and saxophone.  (Percussionist Rebop Kwaku Baah was fired prior to the album's completion, but his contributions are heard on two of its tracks.)  The album uses a broader variety of keyboard instruments than previous Traffic albums, adding Moog to their repertoire.

I Want Home | "I Want Home" is a song by the English rock band Bee Gees, written by Barry Gibb and released as a single in Australia in early 1966, backed with "Cherry Red".  Their last single on Leedon had not been a hit, so the credit "Barry Gibb and the Bee Gees" used on the last several discs now reverted to simply "Bee Gees".  Neither song appeared on any Bee Gees album until the 1967 compilation album, "Turn Around, Look At Us", but both were featured on "Brilliant From Birth" the 1998 anthology of the group's Australian recordings.
response.summary_2: Rosko Gee is a Jamaican bassist, who has played with the English band Traffic on their albums "When the Eagle Flies" (1974) and "The Last Great Traffic Jam" (2005). "When the Eagle Flies" featured Jim Capaldi (drums, keyboards and vocals), Steve Winwood (guitar, keyboards and vocals) and Chris Wood (flute and sax), with percussionist Rebop Kwaku Baah heard on two tracks; "The Last Great Traffic Jam" is a live album/DVD recorded from Traffic's 1994 reunion tour.
response.answer: He played with the English band Traffic. On When the Eagle Flies (1974) the personnel listed include Jim Capaldi (drums, keyboards, vocals), Steve Winwood (guitar, keyboards, vocals) and Chris Wood (flute, sax); percussionist Rebop Kwaku Baah is heard on two tracks. The Last Great Traffic Jam (2005) is a live Traffic album/DVD recorded from the band's 1994 reunion tour (the summaries do not list its specific personnel).
